---
session: "`r session <- 3; session`"
topic: "`r library(tidyverse); topic <- read_csv('../key_dates.csv', name_repair='universal') |> filter(Course.Element == 'Class Session', Item.Number==session) |> pull(Details); topic`"
date: "Last Updated `r Sys.Date()`"
---

$$\newcommand{\E}{\mathbb{E}} \newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}} \newcommand{\bx}{\mathbf{x}}\newcommand{\bbeta}{\mathbf{\beta}} \newcommand{\bX}{\mathbf{X}} \newcommand{\by}{\mathbf{y}} \newcommand{\bz}{\mathbf{z}} \newcommand{\bA}{\mathbf{A}} \newcommand{\bb}{\mathbf{b}} \newcommand{\bc}{\mathbf{c}} \newcommand{\bH}{\mathbf{H}} \newcommand{\bI}{\mathbf{I}} \newcommand{\V}{\mathbb{V}} \newcommand{\argmin}{\text{arg min}}$$

Today: 

- OLS via ERM
  - Loss function + Function class
- Definitions: 
  - Error
  - Accuracy
  - Loss
  - Risk
- Examples
  - Regression
    - MSE
    - LAD
    - MSPE
      - Quickly derive WLS
      - Quadratic forms and definiteness
    - Check loss
  - Classification
    - 0/1 Accuracy
    - TPR, FPR, TNR, FNR
    - Composites
    - Custom loss functions
- Properties of a good loss function
  - Non-negative (bounded?)
  - Smooth
  - Differentiable
  - Convex
    - Argument via Taylor under-approximation
- Looking at MSE
  - Bias, Variance, Irreducible (Bayes) Error
  - Estimation vs prediction error
  - General message about bias and variance
  - BLUE - actually important? 
- Evaluating loss
  - Training/Test
  - Sample splitting
  - CV
  - Boostrapping
- Other loss functions
  - Soft classification
  - Generative models
    - Generative models as MLE (min KL)

Last week, we showed that OLS can be derived purely as a 'loss minimization'
problem, without reference to a specific probabilitsic model. Specifically, we
argued that given: 

- mean squared error as a loss function
- linear predictors as a function class

OLS is the natural and unique algorithm to use. This was a special case of
*empirical risk minimization*: minimizing our loss on the training data. 
This gives us a general principle for building ML systems: once we define the
loss, we can just throw it at a sufficiently interesting set of functions and
pick the minimizer as our predictor. (We will return to the set of functions at 
a later point.)

This suggests that it's worth spending some time thinking about what makes a
_good_ loss function. 

```{r}
#| include: false
1+1
```
